Metadata.title: SaaS Starter | Production-Grade Next.js SaaS Foundation
Metadata.description: Ship a team-based SaaS with auth, roles, seat-based Stripe billing, AI budgeting, i18n, and a hardened security pipeline — all prewired on Next.js 16, Supabase, and React 19.

errors.unableSocialLogin: Unable to continue with social login.

messages.accountCreated: Account created. Check your inbox to verify email if confirmation is enabled.

description.signup: Get started with production-ready auth, teams, and billing.

hero.badge: Ship your product, not your platform
hero.title: The SaaS infrastructure you'd spend months building is already done.
hero.description: Auth, multi-tenant teams, seat-based Stripe billing, AI with DB-enforced budgets, audit logging, and a hardened security pipeline — wired end-to-end on Next.js 16, Supabase, and React 19 so you can focus entirely on what makes your product unique.
hero.shipNowTitle: What you ship on day one
hero.shipItem1: Full auth lifecycle — signup, login, OAuth (Google + Microsoft), password reset, and protected dashboard with session refresh middleware
hero.shipItem2: Multi-tenant teams with invite flows, role-based access (owner/admin/member), atomic ownership transfer, and last-owner database guards
hero.shipItem3: Stripe checkout, billing portal, plan changes, and idempotent webhook processing with a claims table and optional Trigger.dev offload
hero.shipItem4: Seat reconciliation via cron with a retry queue, automatic quantity sync on invite acceptance, and subscription status tracking
hero.shipItem5: AI chat with streaming responses, plan-aware access gating, and monthly token budgets enforced in Postgres with claim/finalize and retry queues
hero.shipItem6: CSRF double-submit cookies, CSP with nonces, typed env with boot validation, request IDs, audit logging, and multi-layer rate limiting with circuit breaker

gettingStarted.title: Running in minutes, not weeks
gettingStarted.description: Three steps from clone to a working SaaS with real auth, teams, billing, and security — backed by four detailed architecture guides.
gettingStarted.step1Text: Install dependencies, copy .env.example, and set your keys. Typed env validation catches misconfigurations at boot, not at runtime.
gettingStarted.step2Text: Run schema.sql in Supabase (20+ tables, RLS policies, triggers, and RPCs are ready), add Stripe price IDs, and set your Resend domain.
gettingStarted.step3Text: Auth, teams, billing, AI, and security are handled. Customize branding, add your product logic, and deploy — Redis, Trigger.dev, and Sentry are opt-in when you need scale.

whyStarter.label: Why this starter
whyStarter.title: Production-grade patterns, not demo-quality scaffolding.
whyStarter.description: Every integration is built the way you'd build it after learning the hard lessons — atomic DB operations, retry queues, idempotent webhooks, and graceful fallbacks throughout.

bestPractices.title: Hardened by default
bestPractices.description: Security, data integrity, and operational resilience are built into every layer — not bolted on as afterthoughts.
bestPractices.securityDefaultsText: CSRF double-submit cookies, CSP with nonces, strict API content-type enforcement, request IDs on every response, and composable auth/role middleware on every route.
bestPractices.teamBillingText: Atomic RPCs for invite acceptance, ownership transfer, and subscription sync. Database triggers prevent deleting the last team owner and auto-repair orphaned team context.
bestPractices.operationalText: Multi-layer rate limiting (Redis, Supabase RPC, in-memory) with circuit breaker fallback, seat reconciliation crons with retry queues, and idempotent Stripe webhook processing.

stack.title: Inside the stack
stack.description: Next.js 16 App Router with React 19, TypeScript strict mode, and clear boundaries between UI routes, API handlers, and shared platform libraries.
stack.authText: Supabase SSR auth with Google and Microsoft OAuth, team context caching (Redis + in-memory + DB), role-based API wrappers, invite acceptance with atomic seat sync, and full account lifecycle management.
stack.billingText: Stripe checkout, portal, and plan-change APIs backed by idempotent webhook processing via a claims table, subscription sync RPCs, seat reconciliation crons, and optional Trigger.dev offload.
stack.opsText: Streaming AI chat with DB-enforced monthly token budgets, Resend support email with sender context, batched audit logging, and optional Intercom and Sentry integrations — all behind feature flags.

pricing.title: Live pricing synced from Stripe
pricing.description: Prices are fetched directly from your Stripe account at render time. Configure your price IDs once and the landing page, checkout, and billing portal stay in sync automatically.

starter.name: Starter
starter.description: Auth, teams, and billing for founders validating a new product.

growth.name: Growth
growth.description: Add AI access, seat scaling, and usage tracking as your team grows.

pro.name: Pro
pro.description: Full platform with priority support, audit logging, and advanced integrations.

ctaFaq.title: Stop rebuilding infrastructure. Start shipping product.
ctaFaq.description: Every SaaS needs auth, teams, billing, and security. Start with the version that took thousands of lines of production-minded code to get right, and put your time into what customers actually pay for.
ctaFaq.q1: What is included out of the box?
ctaFaq.a1: A complete SaaS foundation: Supabase auth with OAuth (Google + Microsoft), multi-tenant teams with role-based access and invite flows, seat-based Stripe billing with idempotent webhooks and reconciliation crons, streaming AI chat with DB-enforced token budgets, support email via Resend, audit logging, i18n in 8 languages, and a security pipeline covering CSRF, CSP, rate limiting, and request tracing.
ctaFaq.q2: How deep are the integrations — stubs or production-ready?
ctaFaq.a2: Production-ready. Stripe webhooks use a claims table for idempotency with optional Trigger.dev offload. Team invites resolve through atomic Postgres RPCs that sync seat quantities. AI budgets are enforced in the database with claim/finalize semantics and retry queues. Rate limiting cascades through three layers with a circuit breaker. These are patterns you'd arrive at after operating a real product.
ctaFaq.q3: How are teams, roles, and permissions handled?
ctaFaq.a3: Every user gets a personal team on signup via a database trigger. Teams support owner, admin, and member roles with invite flows, token-based acceptance, and atomic ownership transfer. Database triggers prevent deleting the last owner and auto-repair orphaned team context. All team API routes use composable middleware that resolves auth, team membership, and role in a single pipeline.
ctaFaq.q4: What can I scale up later without rearchitecting?
ctaFaq.a4: Redis for rate-limit and team-context caching (falls back gracefully without it), Trigger.dev for offloading webhook processing to background jobs, and Sentry for error monitoring — all opt-in behind feature flags. The Postgres schema, RLS policies, and API pipeline are designed to support these without structural changes.
